questions.promptText: Which of the following is NOT a built-in primitive data type in C?
questions.explanation: C has no built-in 'string' type; strings are represented as char arrays.

questions.promptText: What is the output of: print(2 + 3 * 4) in Python?
questions.explanation: Multiplication has higher precedence than addition: 3*4=12, then 2+12=14.

questions.promptText: Which loop is guaranteed to execute at least once?
questions.explanation: A do-while loop checks its condition after the first execution.

questions.promptText: What does the '==' operator check in most programming languages?
questions.explanation: '==' compares two values for equality.

questions.promptText: What is the time complexity of accessing an element in an array by index?
questions.explanation: Array indexing is a direct memory offset calculation, done in constant time.

questions.promptText: Which of the following is a valid variable name in most programming languages?
questions.explanation: Variable names typically can't start with a digit or contain spaces/hyphens.

questions.promptText: What is recursion?
questions.explanation: Recursion is when a function calls itself to solve smaller instances of a problem.

questions.promptText: Which keyword is used to define a constant in C++?
questions.explanation: 'const' declares a variable whose value cannot be changed after initialization.

questions.promptText: What is the output of 5 % 2 in most programming languages?
questions.explanation: The modulo operator returns the remainder of the division: 5/2 leaves remainder 1.

questions.promptText: Which data structure uses LIFO (Last In First Out) order?
questions.explanation: A stack removes the most recently added element first, i.e. LIFO.

questions.promptText: What is the time complexity of binary search on a sorted array of n elements?
questions.explanation: Binary search halves the search space each step, giving O(log n).

questions.promptText: Which sorting algorithm has the best average-case time complexity?
questions.explanation: Merge Sort has O(n log n) average case, better than the O(n^2) of the others listed.

questions.promptText: In a singly linked list, what is the time complexity to insert a node at the beginning?
questions.explanation: Inserting at the head only requires updating the head pointer, a constant-time operation.

questions.promptText: Which data structure is used to implement recursion internally?
questions.explanation: Function calls are pushed onto a call stack, which manages recursive calls.

questions.promptText: What is the worst-case time complexity of QuickSort?
questions.explanation: QuickSort degrades to O(n^2) when pivot selection is consistently poor.

questions.promptText: A complete binary tree with n nodes has a height of approximately:
questions.explanation: A complete binary tree's height grows logarithmically with the number of nodes.

questions.promptText: Which traversal of a binary search tree gives elements in sorted order?
questions.explanation: Inorder traversal (left, root, right) visits BST nodes in ascending order.

questions.promptText: What is the space complexity of an adjacency matrix representation of a graph with V vertices?
questions.explanation: An adjacency matrix stores a VxV grid regardless of the number of edges.

questions.promptText: Which data structure follows FIFO (First In First Out) order?
questions.explanation: A queue processes elements in the order they were added, i.e. FIFO.

questions.promptText: What is the time complexity of inserting an element into a max-heap?
questions.explanation: Inserting requires a 'bubble up' operation proportional to the tree's height.

questions.promptText: In a hash table, what causes a collision?

options.text: Two keys mapping to the same index
A table that is too large
Using a prime number for size

questions.explanation: A collision happens when the hash function maps two different keys to the same slot.

questions.promptText: Which OOP concept allows a class to inherit properties from another class?
questions.explanation: Inheritance lets a class reuse fields and methods from a parent class.

questions.promptText: What is encapsulation in OOP?

options.text: Hiding implementation details and exposing only necessary interfaces

questions.explanation: Encapsulation bundles data and methods while restricting direct access to internals.

questions.promptText: What is method overriding?

options.text: Defining multiple methods with the same name but different parameters in the same class
A subclass providing a specific implementation of a method already defined in its superclass

questions.explanation: Overriding lets a subclass redefine a method inherited from its parent.

questions.promptText: What is method overloading?

options.text: Redefining a method in a subclass
Multiple methods with the same name but different parameter lists in the same class

questions.explanation: Overloading allows multiple methods in the same class to share a name with different signatures.

questions.promptText: Which OOP principle refers to showing only essential features and hiding the details?
questions.explanation: Abstraction focuses on exposing only relevant details to the user.

questions.promptText: Can a constructor be inherited in most OOP languages like Java?
questions.explanation: Constructors are tied to their own class and are not inherited by subclasses.

questions.promptText: What is a virtual function used for (e.g. in C++)?
questions.explanation: Virtual functions enable dynamic dispatch, resolved at runtime based on the actual object type.

questions.promptText: What is an abstract class?

options.text: A class that cannot be instantiated and may contain abstract methods
A class with only static methods

questions.explanation: Abstract classes define a partial implementation meant to be extended, not instantiated directly.

questions.promptText: In Java, which keyword is used to prevent a class from being inherited?
questions.explanation: A class declared 'final' cannot be subclassed.

questions.promptText: What is the main function of an operating system's process scheduler?

options.text: Decide which process runs on the CPU next

questions.explanation: The scheduler determines the order and timing of process execution on the CPU.

questions.promptText: Which of the following is NOT a CPU scheduling algorithm?
questions.explanation: Binary Search is a searching algorithm, not a CPU scheduling algorithm.

questions.promptText: What is a deadlock in an operating system?

options.text: A situation where two or more processes are waiting indefinitely for each other's resources
A process that has finished executing

questions.explanation: A deadlock occurs when processes are stuck waiting on resources held by each other.

questions.promptText: What is the purpose of virtual memory?

options.text: To allow execution of processes larger than physical memory using disk space
To speed up the CPU clock

questions.explanation: Virtual memory uses disk space to extend the effective address space beyond physical RAM.

questions.promptText: Which of the following is a necessary condition for deadlock?
questions.explanation: Mutual exclusion is one of the four Coffman conditions required for deadlock.

questions.promptText: What is a thread?

options.text: The smallest unit of execution within a process

questions.explanation: A thread is a lightweight unit of execution that shares a process's resources.

questions.promptText: What does 'thrashing' refer to in an OS?

options.text: Excessive paging causing high overhead and low CPU utilization

questions.explanation: Thrashing occurs when a system spends more time paging than executing useful work.

questions.promptText: Which memory management technique divides memory into fixed-size blocks?
questions.explanation: Paging divides memory into equal-sized fixed blocks called pages/frames.

questions.promptText: What is a semaphore used for?

options.text: Synchronizing access to shared resources among processes

questions.explanation: A semaphore is a synchronization tool used to control access to shared resources.

questions.promptText: What does DNS stand for?
questions.explanation: DNS translates human-readable domain names into IP addresses.

questions.promptText: Which layer of the OSI model is responsible for routing?
questions.explanation: The Network layer handles logical addressing and routing of packets.

questions.promptText: What is the default port number for HTTP?
questions.explanation: HTTP traffic conventionally uses port 80 by default.

questions.promptText: Which protocol is used to send email?
questions.explanation: SMTP (Simple Mail Transfer Protocol) is used for sending email.

questions.promptText: What does TCP guarantee that UDP does not?
questions.explanation: TCP is connection-oriented and ensures reliable, ordered delivery, unlike UDP.

questions.promptText: What is the purpose of an IP address?

options.text: To identify a device uniquely on a network

questions.explanation: An IP address uniquely identifies a device on a network for communication.

questions.promptText: What is the main goal of unit testing?

options.text: Test individual components or functions in isolation

questions.explanation: Unit testing verifies that individual pieces of code work correctly in isolation.

questions.promptText: What is regression testing?

options.text: Testing a system for the first time
Re-testing a system after changes to ensure existing functionality still works

questions.explanation: Regression testing confirms that recent changes haven't broken existing functionality.

questions.promptText: What is the difference between black-box and white-box testing?

options.text: Black-box tests internal code structure, white-box tests functionality without knowing internals
Black-box tests functionality without knowing internal code, white-box tests based on internal code structure

questions.explanation: Black-box testing focuses on inputs/outputs; white-box testing examines internal logic.

questions.promptText: What is a test case?

options.text: A set of conditions and inputs used to determine if a system works as expected

questions.explanation: A test case specifies inputs, execution steps, and expected results for verification.

questions.promptText: What is the purpose of integration testing?

options.text: To test the interaction between combined modules or components

questions.explanation: Integration testing verifies that different modules work correctly together.

questions.promptText: What does 'code coverage' measure?

options.text: The percentage of source code executed during testing
The number of lines of code written

questions.explanation: Code coverage quantifies how much of the source code is exercised by tests.

questions.promptText: What is a 'false positive' in testing?

options.text: A test that incorrectly reports a failure when there isn't one
A test that takes too long

questions.explanation: A false positive incorrectly flags working code as broken.

questions.promptText: What is smoke testing?

options.text: A quick, preliminary test to check if the critical functions of a build work

questions.explanation: Smoke testing is a fast sanity check on core functionality before deeper testing.

questions.promptText: In Agile development, when is testing typically performed?

options.text: Only at the end of the project

questions.explanation: Agile emphasizes continuous testing integrated throughout each development cycle.

questions.promptText: Which SQL keyword is used to retrieve data from a database?
questions.explanation: SELECT is the standard SQL statement for querying data.

questions.promptText: What does 'PRIMARY KEY' ensure in a database table?

options.text: Unique identification of each row and no NULL values

questions.explanation: A primary key uniquely identifies each row and disallows NULLs.

questions.promptText: Which SQL clause is used to filter records?
questions.explanation: The WHERE clause filters rows based on specified conditions.

questions.promptText: What is normalization in database design?

options.text: Organizing data to reduce redundancy and improve integrity

questions.explanation: Normalization restructures tables to minimize redundancy and dependency issues.

questions.promptText: Which type of JOIN returns all records from both tables, with NULLs where there is no match?
questions.explanation: A FULL OUTER JOIN combines all rows from both tables, filling unmatched sides with NULL.

questions.promptText: What does ACID stand for in the context of database transactions?
questions.explanation: ACID describes the four key guarantees of reliable database transactions.
